PROJ_LGL_NAME: Building Resilient Digital Infrastructure for Growth - BRIDGE
PROJ_DISPLAY_NAME: Building Resilient Digital Infrastructure for Growth - BRIDGE
PROJ_DEV_OBJECTIVE_DESC: To expand inclusive use of high quality and climate resilient broadband internet in selected unserved and underserved areas of Nigeria
SECT_LONG_NAME_LIST: Digital Government - Public Administration;Digital Infrastructure
PROJ_ABSTRACT_TEXT: The BRIDGE project directly supports Nigeria’s broadband strategy and digital transformation agenda by expanding access to high-quality, climate-resilient broadband infrastructure in underserved areas. With a total budget of US$1.6 billion—including US$500 million in IDA financing and US$1.1 billion in expected private capital—it will establish a Special Purpose Vehicle (SPV) to deploy 90,000 km of open-access fiber. Public funds will support feasibility studies, transaction advisory, SPV setup, and equity contributions. The network will be energy-efficient, climate-adaptive, and powered by renewable energy where needed.BRIDGE is designed to address structural market failures that have left much of Nigeria without reliable broadband access. It targets areas where private sector investment has not been viable, using catalytic public financing to unlock new infrastructure development and crowd in commercial capital. By improving the reach and resilience of broadband networks, the project lays the foundation for inclusive economic growth, supports digital public service delivery, and enhances connectivity for critical sectors such as education, health, and local governance.The project includes two components. Component 1: Resilient Digital Infrastructure will finance the rollout of backbone and middle-mile fiber networks, crowding in private investment through the SPV. It will fund preparatory studies, transaction advisory, and equity issuance, enabling a phased, demand-responsive deployment of 90,000 km of open-access, climate-resilient fiber. The SPV will offer wholesale services to ISPs under transparent, non-discriminatory terms, with a strong emphasis on commercial viability, environmental standards, and energy efficiency. The fiber network will replace microwave backhaul, reducing emissions and improving reliability, and will be co-located with renewable energy investments where grid access is limited.Component 2: Project Management and Implementation Support will finance a dedicated Project Implementation Unit (PIU) within the Federal Ministry of Communications, Innovation & Digital Economy (FMCIDE). The PIU will oversee procurement, safeguards, financial management, and monitoring and evaluation activities. The component will also support citizen engagement, including grievance redress, and ensure quality assurance and audit functions are maintained throughout implementation.

PROJ_LGL_NAME: Nigeria: Primary Healthcare Provision Strengthening Program
PROJ_DISPLAY_NAME: Nigeria: Primary Healthcare Provision Strengthening Program
PROJ_DEV_OBJECTIVE_DESC: The Program Development Objective is to improve utilization of quality essential health care services and health system resilience in the Federal Republic of Nigeria.
TEAM_LEAD_NAME_LIST: Olumide Olaolu Okunola (ADM);Maxwell Bruku Dapaah;Ritgak Tilley Gyado
SECT_LONG_NAME_LIST: Public Administration - Health;Health;Health Facilities and Construction
PROJ_ABSTRACT_TEXT: Nigeria is Africa’s largest country (over 200 million people) and largest economy (nominal gross domestic product (GDP) of around US$405 billion in 2020). With an abundance of resources and a young and dynamic society, it has the potential to be a giant on the global stage. But with over 40 percent of its population (over 80 million people) in poverty, Nigeria is also among the countries with the largest number of people living below the poverty line. The HOPE Program aims to support the Governments program to achieve results in human capital development in Nigeria. The operation will be open to all the 36 states in Nigeria and the Federal Capital Territory (FCT). The planned Program will be managed through a sector-wide program pooling funds (Government  and Development Partners) and     aims to providing cost effective essential benefit package of services focusing on reproductive health, maternal care and nutrition, childhood illnesses, and non-communicable disease screening. A discrete package of promotive, preventive, and simple curative interventions will be delivered at the community level, complementing facility-based primary health care services. The estimated cost of achieving full coverage of 57 primary care services under the Basic Health Care Provision Program (BHCPP) is US$14 per capita annually, more than currently available government resources. Achieving this goal will therefore require careful consideration of the pace of the planned scale-up and of service priorities. To consolidate investments, the BHCPP proposes to: (i) expand Primary Health Centers (PHCs) from one per ward to a population-responsive distribution of two per ward, on average, with a total of over 17,000  nationwide; and (ii) support the implementation of Basic and Comprehensive Emergency Obstetric and Newborn Care (BEmONC and CEmONC), including one CEmONC per Local Government Area (LGA), for a total of 774, and an equitable distribution of Tier 2  BEmONC facilities linked to each of the CEmONC facilities. The BHCPP leverages the opportunity to strengthen emergency medical response in rural areas by addressing transportation barriers. In addition, the BHCPP includes plans for workforce strengthening, including investments in training curricula, to address personnel gaps and improve competency and skills. The BHCPP will leverage digital health to create an integrated ecosystem and supports the development of a digital backbone, ensuring interoperability and data exchange. The IPF Component will mainly finance technical assistance (TA)  that support the achievement of Program results, including: maternal mortality reduction innovation initiative to support pilot interventions to address maternal mortality in high-burden states; the hiring of an Independent Verification Agency; the design, procurement, and deployment of a federated digital-in-health enterprise architecture; operationalization of the public health fellows' program; and a platform for coordinated TA to support Program implementation. The IPF component will provide support to both national and subnational entities.

PROJ_LGL_NAME: Sustainable Power and Irrigation for Nigeria Project
PROJ_DISPLAY_NAME: Sustainable Power and Irrigation for Nigeria Project
PROJ_DEV_OBJECTIVE_DESC: The Project Development Objective is to strengthen dam safety and improve management of water resources for hydropower and irrigation in selected areas of Nigeria.
TEAM_LEAD_NAME_LIST: Zacharias Michael Ziegelhoefer (ADM);Pravin Karki;Emmanuel Chinedu Umolu
SECT_LONG_NAME_LIST: Irrigation and Drainage;Energy Generation - Hydro;Other - Energy and Mineral Resources;Public Administration - Water, Sanitation, and Solid Waste Management ;Water Resources
PROJ_ABSTRACT_TEXT: The proposed Sustainable Power and Irrigation in Nigeria (SPIN) project will address nexus of water-food-energy challenges in Nigeria through investments in irrigation, dam safety, and hydropower planning. By mobilizing water for productive purposes, optimizing the use of existing storage facilities, and enhancing hydropower planning capacity, the project will make integrated management of water, energy and food more effective and resilient in Nigeria.  It will do this by investing in the management and resilience of multipurpose water infrastructure as well as strengthening the policies, institutions, capacities, and inter-governmental cooperation required. The proposed project includes four main components:1) Institutional strengthening and capacity building for water resources management; 2) Irrigation Modernization; 3) Improvements in Dam Operations and enhancing dam safety; and 4) Project Management.

PROJ_LGL_NAME: Nigeria Human Capital Opportunities for Prosperity and Equity (HOPE) - Governance
PROJ_DISPLAY_NAME: Nigeria Human Capital Opportunities for Prosperity and Equity (HOPE) - Governance
PROJ_DEV_OBJECTIVE_DESC: To support the Federal Republic of Nigeria in strengthening financial and human resource management in basic education and primary heath care sectors.
TEAM_LEAD_NAME_LIST: Ikechukwu John Nweje (ADM);Shinsaku Nomura;Olumide Olaolu Okunola
SECT_LONG_NAME_LIST: Sub-National Government;Public Administration - Education;Health
PROJ_ABSTRACT_TEXT: HOPE-GOV is a PforR (hybrid) and is one of a series of three interconnected and complementary Programs strategically positioned to address different challenges, but all geared towards helping achieve the same set of ultimate outcomes. While the other two - HOPE-Primary Healthcare (HOPE PHC) and HOPE-Basic Education (HOPE-BED) will focus on the quantity and quality of services delivered at the facility level, HOPE-Governance (HOPE-GOV) focuses on the critical crosscutting challenges and enabling factors related to both financial and human resource management. The proposed Program is expected to support improvements in the strategic allocation and expenditure of resources for basic education and primary health through: (a) strengthened predictability and efficiency of financial flows and planning and budgeting processes; (b) improved transparency and accountability of financial transfers and expenditure; (c) addressing critical human resource shortfalls and improving equitable deployment. The Program implementation will involve relevant sector Ministries, Departments and Agencies at the federal and state tiers of government in  all 36 states and the FCT. The proposed Program will focus on the following result areas:•	Results Area 1: Increased availability and effectiveness of financing for basic education and primary health care        service delivery. •	Results Area 2: Enhanced transparency and accountability for basic education and primary health care financing. •	Results Area 3: Improved recruitment, deployment, and performance management of basic education teachers        and primary health workers.The IPF (TA) component will finance, following the World Bank procurement guidelines and regulations, a set of technical assistance activities both at the federal and state levels aimed at (i) strengthening the capacities of federal and state governments for program implementation, coordination, (ii)) Program Coordination and fiduciary management, and (iii) Verification of Results and Monitoring and Evaluation.  The first part will be the largest, focusing on providing capacity support to all participating state governments to strengthen their systems and capacities to enable them to achieve the Program Implementation. The approximate value for the IPF component will be US$ 20 million.

PROJ_LGL_NAME: Nigeria Sustainable Agricultural Value-Chains for Growth (AGROW) Project
PROJ_DISPLAY_NAME: Nigeria Sustainable Agricultural Value-Chains for Growth (AGROW) Project
PROJ_DEV_OBJECTIVE_DESC: To increase smallholder productivity and strengthen targeted agricultural value chains in participating states of Nigeria.
TEAM_LEAD_NAME_LIST: Hardwick Tchale (ADM);Rami Mikko Ahmed Galal;Manievel Sene
SECT_LONG_NAME_LIST: Agricultural Extension, Research, and Other Support Activities ;Crops;Irrigation and Drainage;Public Administration - Agriculture, Fishing, and Forestry ;Agricultural Markets, Commercialization, Agri-Business and Agriculture finance ;Forestry
PROJ_ABSTRACT_TEXT: The proposed project aims to address the critical challenges that affect competitiveness and resilience of agricultural value-chains, and thus stifle the prospects for value-added growth and job creation. In summary these key challenges include: (i) low institutional capacity and non-conducive policies that affect private sector (including farmers') participation in key value-chains; (ii) structural constraints that affect sector modernization and productivity and thus stifle private sector and farmers' returns and quality of jobs from agricultural value-chains; and (iii) poor integration of farmers to markets. The proposed project will therefore focus on interventions that directly or indirectly contribute towards Nigeria's agricultural sector modernization, productivity and value-added growth, including improved food and nutrition security in line with the country's Agriculture Promotion Policy (APP), National Agricultural Technology and Innovation Policy (NATIP). Nigeria's priorities as highlighted in these policy documents is to achieve self-sufficiency in key commodities, transform agriculture into a business-oriented sector, and ensure food security, while also promoting sustainable income and job growth. This operation will also be aligned to the Bank's Country Partnership Framework (CPF) as well as the recent aspirations highlighted in the Global Food and Nutrition Security (FNS) Program and the Mission Agri-connect.

PROJ_LGL_NAME: Fostering Inclusive Finance for MSMEs in Nigeria (FINCLUDE) Project
PROJ_DISPLAY_NAME: Fostering Inclusive Finance for MSMEs in Nigeria (FINCLUDE) Project
PROJ_DEV_OBJECTIVE_DESC: To mobilize private capital and expand the use of inclusive and innovative financial products by micro, small and medium enterprises (MSMEs) in Nigeria.
TEAM_LEAD_NAME_LIST: Hadija Kamayo (ADM);Carlos Leonardo Vicente;Sarmad Ahmed Shaikh
SECT_LONG_NAME_LIST: Banking Institutions;Capital Markets;Public Administration - Financial Sector
PROJ_ABSTRACT_TEXT: The proposed FINCLUDE Project leverages the platforms of the Development Bank of Nigeria (DBN) and its subsidiary, the Impact Credit Guarantee Limited (ICGL), to drive inclusive MSME finance. Through these catalytic institutions, the Project will deploy a package of complementary, inclusive, and innovative instruments, tailored to the diverse needs of MSMEs in Nigeria. Designed as an Investment Project Financing (IPF) with performance-based conditions (PBCs), the Project comprises three interrelated components aimed at delivering inclusive and innovative financial and non-financial solutions tailored to Nigeria’s diverse MSME landscape. Each component leverages a complementary mix of market-based instruments designed to: crowd-in private capital, test market innovations, and promote financial sustainability. They include: Component 1: Inclusive and Innovative MSME Finance Products, with 2 sub-components: (i) Sub-component 1.1 : Catalytic Financing for MSMEs through the provision of Tier 2 subordinated capital to eligible Participating Financial Institutions (PFIs) and expand their capacity to lend to MSMEs in Nigeria; and (ii) Sub-component 1.2: MSME Capital Market Access to support the design, establishment, capitalization, and operationalization of an MSME Investment Fund for the provision of: (a) equity and quasi-equity financing to eligible MSMEs; and (b) long-term debt to eligible MSMEs. Component 2: De-risking and Mobilizing Private Capital for MSMEs through the capitalization of ICGL to provide Partial Credit Guarantees (PCG) for loans issued by PFIs to MSMEs. Component 3: Technical Assistance, composed of two sub-components: (i) Sub-component 3.1: Modernizing and Digitizing the Ecosystem to finance targeted TA investments to enhance structural efficiency and promote digitization and modernization across the MSME finance ecosystem. The modernization effort will focus on strengthening integration and responsiveness across the three key interrelated actors in the MSME finance chain, i.e. DBN-PFIs-MSMEs; and (ii) Sub-component 3.2: Project Management and Implementation Support to strengthen implementation capacity, including the engagement of additional dedicated technical personnel; oversight, fiduciary management, compliance, and results tracking.

PROJ_LGL_NAME: National Social Safety Net Program-Scale Up
PROJ_DISPLAY_NAME: National Social Safety Net Program-Scale Up
PROJ_DEV_OBJECTIVE_DESC: Expand coverage of shock responsive safety net support among the poor and vulnerable and strengthen the national safety net delivery system.
SECT_LONG_NAME_LIST: FY17 - Social Protection;FY17 - Public Administration - Social Protection
PROJ_ABSTRACT_TEXT: Nigeria has notably high levels of poverty and vulnerability. Predictions indicate that the ongoing global economic crisis induced by the COVID-19 pandemic has contributed to a worsening of these levels. The latest official data released by the National Bureau ofStatistics (NBS) show that around 4 in 10 Nigerians (82.9 million people) were living below the national poverty line in 2018/19. Moreover, with forecasted contraction of economy by around 4% in 2020, the poverty headcount rate is estimated to increase from 40.1 % in 2019 to 44% in 2022. This is due to a combination of the COVID-19 crisis and high levels of inflation (16. % in January 2021) with rise in food prices accounting for 63% rise in inflation. This means close to a 100 million Nigerians (about 20 million HHs) could fall below the poverty line by 2022, absent any government measures.The Government of Nigeria (GoN) is considering further expansion of its National Social Safety Net Program (NASSP)- P151488 to provide cash transfers support to the existing and new poor and vulnerable populations to mitigate the short term impact of the current and future shocks including COVID-19 pandemic and recent surge in inflation. The proposed NASSP Scale Up (NASSP SU) will complement the government plan by providing financial resources and technical support for the delivery of temporary cash transfer support to the population affected by shocks, to protect the livelihoods of the existing chronically poor households as well as by strengthening the shock responsive national social protection system.The levels of social protection in Nigeria, both in terms of financing and coverage, has been very low. Apart from low coverage, the performance of the government social safety net programs is variable is also low. The limited existing social protection programs in the country also remains fragmented. Significant and persistent gender gaps exist in Nigeria, including in access to social protection services. There has been recent progress which shows the government’s commitment towards providing stronger social protection to its citizens. The government is in the process of scaling up social protection in response to COVID-19. The establishment of the RRR and the launch of the COVID-CT required innovations on several fronts. Phase II of the project intends to expand and build upon the activities conducted through the NASSP. The World Bank has been a key partner in the government’s effort to expand and strengthen the social protection programs and systems, particularly through its support to different national programs.The overall objective of the NASSP SU is to expand coverage of shock responsive safety net support among the poor and vulnerable and strengthen the national safety net delivery system. Gender and citizen engagement are important dimension of the results to be achieved by the project. These will be monitored through the outcome indicators as well as intermediate indicators looking at specific aspects of gender and citizen engagement.The project builds on the NASSP to provide strengthened social assistance support to Nigerian citizens and will support the following components:Component 1. Economic Shock Responsive Cash Transfer (ESR-CT). US$ 600 million; This component will provide temporary cash transfer to eligible beneficiaries in response to the current economic shocks affecting the country. The component has two subcomponents; i) Subcomponent 1.1. Rural ESR-CT to eligible beneficiaries in the NSR, ii) Subcomponent 1.2. Urban ESR-CT to eligible beneficiaries through the expansion of the RRR.Component 2. This component has been dropped. Component 3. Delivery System Strengthening and Program management. US$ 53 million. Component 3 will expand and strengthen the social assistance/safety nets delivery systems through increased integration and use of digital technologies at various stages of the delivery chain and also provide project management support. The Component will support supports two subcomponents; I) Subcomponent 3.1. Expansion and strengthening of the safety nets delivery systems through increased integration and the use of digital technologies, I) Subcomponent 3.2. Project management support.In addition to the three-components discussed above, the project will also support: a) citizen engagement and social accountability, b) gender and social inclusion, an c) climate co-benefit, The primary beneficiaries of the project will be the cash transfer beneficiaries. In addition to cash transfer beneficiaries, the project will improve the delivery systems for social assistance programs – both current and future.The proposed project is aligned with the Country Partnership Framework (CPF) for the Federal Republic of Nigeria FY 21 – FY 25 (World Bank Report #153873-NG). The CPF has four strategic Pillars: (i) strengthening the foundations of the public sector; (ii) investing in human capital; (iii) promoting jobs and economic transformation and diversification; and (iv) enhancing resilience. The project will contribute to most of the pillars of the CPF. Most directly, the expansion of the CT programs contributes towards increasing the coverage and effectiveness of social assistance, a core objective under Pillar 2. It also contributes to other core objectives of improving investments in human capital under Pillar 2. In addition, the strengthening of social assistance delivery system will strengthen the foundations of the public sector (Pillar 1); the productive inclusion support provided under the project promotes jobs (Pillar 3); and the proposed interventions to mitigate the impact of shocks will enhance resilience (Pillar 4).

PROJ_LGL_NAME: Accelerating Nutrition Results in Nigeria 2.0
PROJ_DISPLAY_NAME: Accelerating Nutrition Results in Nigeria 2.0
PROJ_DEV_OBJECTIVE_DESC: Increase utilization of quality, cost-effective, convergent, nutrition-specific and nutrition-sensitive services for pregnant and lactating women, adolescent girls and children under five years of age in select areas of the recipient's territory.
SECT_LONG_NAME_LIST: Other - Agriculture, Fishing, and Forestry ;Public Administration - Health;Health
PROJ_ABSTRACT_TEXT: Nigeria is Africa’s largest country (over 200 million people) and largest economy (nominal gross domestic product (GDP) of around US$405 billion in 2020). Endowed with an abundance of resources and a young and dynamic society, it has the potential to be a giant on the global stage. However, with over 40 percent of its population (over 80 million people) living in poverty and a Human Capital Index of 0.36, the country harbors the largest number of people living below the poverty line and some of the worst Human Capital Indices. Foundational to this, Nigeria grapples with a daunting triple burden of malnutrition with stunting rates ranking bottom 10 globally. Nigeria accounts for the second largest number of stunted children in the world, and the largest in Sub-Saharan Africa; a quarter of the global figure of wasted children and extremely high proportions of micronutrient deficiencies. In 2024, the Food and Nutrition Security situation in Nigeria has worsened with at least 31.8 million people projected to be in crisis or emergency phases indicating household food consumption gaps in and addressing food needs through negative coping strategies. The situation confounds an already severe baseline of malnutrition with wasting rates at 7% and stunting rates at 37% and indicated to be regressing emanating from food insecurity, poor access to healthcare, climate shocks, conflict and poverty. Such a growing food and nutrition crisis threatens future economic growth by constraining human capital accumulation. Addressing this protracted development challenge will require concerted and coordinate action through a whole-of-government and whole-of society approach that tackles multidimensional drivers.The Accelerating Nutrition Results in Nigeria Project was designed as the first slice of a long-term engagement of the Government of Nigeria, to tackle malnutrition. Performance-Based Contracts supported the the delivery of a Basic Package of Nutrition Services comprising of evidence-based, high impact, prioritized interventions to improve maternal, adolescent and child nutrition. A complementary package of maternal nutrition services were rolled out through primary healthcare centres. The operation also tested the multisectoral convergence of nutrition interventions whilst incentivizing government channels for improved coordination, stewardship and accountability. The positive results from the ANRiN operation are indicative of the potential impact that could be recorded if interventions are prioritized, targeted and scaled in the right geographies.ANRiN 2.0 will draw lessons from the first operation to scale and sustain the foundation laid for nutrition programming through two components. Component 1 will focus on the deployment of a Basic Package of Nutrition Services in primary healthcare facilities and at community level. Component 2 which will scale-up multisectoral convergence interventions that promote food availability, improve household dietary diversity and build resilience across key sectors. Behaviour change interventions will straddle both Components 1 and 2. Component 3 will support multisectoral coordination and accountability actions.

PROJ_LGL_NAME: Nigeria Distributed Access through Renewable Energy Scale-up Project
PROJ_DISPLAY_NAME: Nigeria Distributed Access through Renewable Energy Scale-up Project
PROJ_DEV_OBJECTIVE_DESC: The project development objective (PDO) is to increase access to electricity services for households, public institutions and MSMEs with private sector led distributed renewable energy generation.
EMERGENCY_RESPONSE_CODE: Financing not related to a Response Type
SECT_LONG_NAME_LIST: FY17 - Public Administration - Energy and Extractives;FY17 - Energy Transmission and Distribution;FY17 - Renewable Energy Solar;FY17 - Other Energy and Extractives
PROJ_ABSTRACT_TEXT: Nigeria DARES is envisioned as a scalable platform for achieving universal electrification and supporting energy transition through private sector investment. The platform aims to provide – (i) basic electrification (multiple lighting, radio, and mobile phone charging ability) to 15 million unserved, rural, and remote Nigerians through the deployment of standalone solar, and (ii) grid equivalent electrification via mini grids to over 1.6 million Nigerians, in rural and peri urban settings. The platform will also support Nigeria’s clean energy transition by increasing the reliability of electricity supply for over 2.8 million existing electricity consumers, in peri urban and urban areas, by connecting them to cleaner and more reliable interconnected mini grids that will help replace approximately 570,000 diesel/gasoline gensets. The platform will also support the electrification of federal and state public institutions, such as healthcare and educational facilities, with distributed renewable energy (DRE) under an energy-as-a-service business model to crowd in private sector investment and ensure sustainable operation and maintenance (O&M). The platform will also provide an estimated 295,000 micro, small and medium enterprises (including around 150,000 agri-businesses) with reliable and clean electricity for productive uses that will help improve their economic output, create local jobs, and improve the financial sustainability of mini grids. The focus of the program is on scaling up mini grids and SAS deployment in Nigeria.The project supports the following components:Component 1. Solar Hybrid Mini Grids for Economic Development will support the development of privately owned and operated solar hybrid mini grids in unserved (primarily rural and remote) and underserved (primarily urban and peri-urban) areas that have high economic growth potential. It will also support the electrification of public institutions at the federal and sub-national level such as healthcare and educational facilities. The mini grid component consists of two investment sub-components defined by the different approaches they follow and will be implemented in parallel: Sub-component 1.1-  minimum subsidy tender and sub-component 1.2- performance-based grant program.Component 2: Standalone Solar Systems (SAS) for Households, MSMEs, and agribusinesses will expand availability and affordability of standalone solar systems (SAS) for households, MSMEs, and agribusinesses in rural areas. Through targeted and competitively awarded performance based grants, the uptake of 3 million quality-certified solar home systems (SHS) and 75,000 PUE products in rural areas and amongst lower income segments will be promoted. Furthermore, approximately 10,500 solar solutions will be provided under an energy-as-a-service business model targeting MSMEs, economic clusters such as markets, plazas, trading centres, and agro-processing zones. The performance based grant (PBG) approach will be utilized for this Component.  Component 3. Technical Assistance will support the creation of a framework for rural electrification upscaling, support project implementation, as well as broad capacity building at REA, NERC, FMPWH and other relevant stakeholders. It includes three sub-components: Institutional Strengthening and Policy Reform, Supporting DARES Ecosystem and Implementation, and Engagement with States.

PROJ_DEV_OBJECTIVE_DESC: To institutionalize Women Affinity Groups (WAGs) and other platforms for women’s economic empowerment and enhance the economic opportunities of unbanked women.
SECT_LONG_NAME_LIST: FY17 - Social Protection;FY17 - Public Administration - Social Protection
PROJ_ABSTRACT_TEXT: NFWP-SU will facilitate improved livelihoods and access to financial and select public services for women through targeted assistance following key principles of inclusion, objective targeting, transparency, and accountability, enabling culture, and openness to learning. The project aims to reach beneficiaries with financial and livelihood enhancement services bundled with other vulnerability reduction services like food and nutrition security, RMNCH, and WASH. NFWP will support the enhancement of voice and participation in decision making at the household and community level, responding to the demands of the participating women. The project will focus on creating an enabling environment for women to overcome institutional failures (including market) and barriers to enhance productive livelihoods and socioeconomic advancement. To accomplish the PDO, the project will develop and strengthen new and existing WAGs and Federation with a strong livelihood focus to enhance household income.

PROJ_LGL_NAME: Rural Access and Agricultural Marketing Project - Scale Up
PROJ_DISPLAY_NAME: Rural Access and Agricultural Marketing Project - Scale Up
PROJ_DEV_OBJECTIVE_DESC: The project objective is to improve rural access and climate resilience of communities in targeted rural areas and strengthen institutional capacity for management of the rural road network and in case of an Eligible Crisis or Emergency, respond promptly or effectively to it.
SECT_LONG_NAME_LIST: Public Administration - Transportation;Rural and Inter-Urban Roads
PROJ_ABSTRACT_TEXT: The Rural Access and Agricultural Marketing Project (RAAMP) Scale Up (SU) continues and expands support provided under the series of rural road access projects in Nigeria, leveraging the previously supported rural road asset management and its institutional framework. The project will scale-up support provided to the rural connectivity agenda in the country through the Rural Access and Mobility Project Phase 1 RAMP-1 (P072644), the Second Rural Access and Mobility programs or RAMP-2 (P095003), and the ongoing Nigeria Rural Access and Agricultural Marketing Project (P163353) financed by the World Bank and the Agence Française de Developpement (AFD), as well as the Nigeria Access to Agri Markets project, recently approved by European investment Bank (EIB).The RAAMP SU Project consists of three mutually reinforcing components to support improvements in Nigeria’s rural road network. The first will support direct rehabilitation and upgrades of 3000 km of resilient rural roads, integrating road safety consideration, and building climate resilience. The second will support climate resilient asset management, including supporting operations and maintenance of an additional 3500 km of rural roads and a climate-risk informed road asset management system. Finally, the third component will support institutional transformation and project management. Project activities also include mechanisms to support road safety management, digitalized outcome monitoring, and skills development for management of rural roads as well as gender-targeted opportunities.All states will be eligible to participate in the RAAMP-SU operation subject to meeting specific criteria to demonstrate the state’s commitment to institutional reforms and sustainable financing. Candidate roads from participating states will be assessed according to economic, social, and climate vulnerability criteria as part of the selection and prioritization process. As with the preceding RAAMP project, the Federal Ministry of Agriculture and Food Security will continue to be the project federal counterpart, ensuring project implementation at the federal level through an entity to be created within the newly established National Agriculture Development Fund. State rural access road agencies will be responsible for project implementation at the state level.

PROJ_LGL_NAME: Nigeria: CBN Technical Assistance (TA) Facility
PROJ_DISPLAY_NAME: Nigeria: CBN Technical Assistance (TA) Facility
PROJ_DEV_OBJECTIVE_DESC: To strengthen the CBN’s technology-enabled and data-driven supervision and oversight across the banking, non-banking, payments, and remittance ecosystems in Nigeria.
SECT_LONG_NAME_LIST: Banking Institutions;Public Administration - Financial Sector;Digital Government - Public Administration;Digital Infrastructure;Central Government (Central Agencies)
PROJ_ABSTRACT_TEXT: The CBN Technical Assistance (TA) Facility aims to strengthen the CBN’s technology-enabled and data-driven supervision and oversight across Nigeria’s banking, non-banking, payments, and remittance ecosystems. The Facility will integrate advanced data and digital tools into regulatory and supervisory processes. Beyond technology upgrades, the TA Facility supports a strategic shift toward institutional and supervisory modernization and evidence-based decision-making. The operation is fully aligned with the forthcoming FY26–32 Country Partnership Framework, which prioritizes a sound, resilient, and deep financial sector for inclusive growth and jobs, and with Nigeria’s National Development Plan 2026–2030 by institutionalizing data-driven policy, strengthening CBN–fiscal agency coordination, and expanding access to finance through more effective, risk-based oversight.Component 1: Supervision and Regulation Through Better Data and TechnologyThis component supports the adoption of: (i) Supervisory Technology (SupTech): tailored technology-based solutions that strengthen supervisory processes and risk based oversight and (ii) Digital Rulebook: a structured, technology driven solution that enhances clarity, consistency, and accessibility of regulatory framework for supervisors, financial institutions and other stakeholders. This transition will enable the CBN to become a regional leader and global fast follower in tech-enabled financial supervision. This is particularly critical given the increasing complexity of Nigeria’s financial landscape, characterized by the rapid evolution of financial services offered by institutions both within and beyond the CBN’s regulatory perimeter, including FinTech’s, digital lenders, and virtual asset platforms. Component 2: Payment Systems Infrastructure, Oversight, and ResilienceThis component supports the design of tools, technologies, and oversight frameworks to strengthen the CBN’s capacity to oversee an increasingly complex payments ecosystem, ensure robust governance and supervision of Payment Service Providers (PSPs), Mobile Money Operators (MMO) and Payment Service Banks (PSBs), and safeguard financial stability, consumer protection, and confidence in digital payments.  Component 3: Remittance Systems and Foreign Exchange (FX) Flow MonitoringThis component supports the design and implementation of analytical tools and data systems to strengthen Nigeria’s remittance ecosystem and improve the monitoring and measurement of foreign exchange (FX) flows. The aim is to deepen understanding of remittance flows, enhance the quality and granularity of FX data, and support evidence-based policy formulation and Balance of Payments (BoP) reporting. By supporting the design of digital-only remittance solutions, the component also seeks to provide insight on strategies to channel more remittance flows through formal systems.
